title: Closed-form Bayesian quantum estimation of Gaussian states
abstract: Bayesian quantum estimation provides a robust framework for quantum technologies, especially in scenarios with limited data and minimal prior information. Yet, its application to continuous-variable Gaussian systems has remained limited and largely numerical due to the complexity of the underlying parameter integrals. Here, we introduce a variational framework reducing the optimisation over measurements and estimators to a finite-dimensional linear problem and admitting closed-form solutions. This is achieved by restricting the analysis to operators polynomial in the canonical quadratures, leading to solutions with a geometric interpretation as orthogonal projections of the global optimum. We further derive a necessary and sufficient condition for global optimality. Through single-shot examples, we show that the framework yields experimentally feasible strategies based on Gaussian operations and quadrature measurements that are either optimal or near-optimal, and that replacing the induced estimator with the posterior mean further improves performance towards the global optimum.
summary: This paper introduces a closed-form method for Bayesian quantum estimation of Gaussian states by approximating the optimal operator within a finite-dimensional polynomial subspace. It transforms a complex, infinite-dimensional optimization problem into a tractable linear algebra problem. The framework allows for the design of near-optimal, experimentally feasible measurement strategies for tasks like displacement and squeezing estimation.
main_problem: The computational intractability of Bayesian quantum estimation for continuous-variable Gaussian states due to infinite-dimensional optimization and complex parameter integrals.
main_result: A variational framework that reduces the optimization of measurements and estimators to a finite-dimensional linear problem with closed-form solutions using polynomial subspaces.
method: The authors use a variational approach restricting operators to a polynomial subspace of canonical quadratures, transforming the Mean Square Loss minimization into a solvable linear system involving a Gram matrix.
model_or_system: Continuous-variable (CV) quantum systems consisting of N bosonic modes, specifically focusing on Gaussian states characterized by mean vectors and covariance matrices.
key_observables: Canonical quadratures (q and p), Symmetric Posterior Mean (SPM) operator, and Weyl symbols.
important_parameters: Displacement parameter, squeezing parameter, prior variance (Gaussian and uniform), and thermal photon number.
main_assumptions: The input states are Gaussian; the optimization is restricted to a polynomial subspace; the Gaussian state is assumed to be faithful.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares relative Mean Squared Loss for different polynomial estimators (linear, cubic) and the posterior mean in displacement estimation. Figure 2 shows the convergence of constrained and posterior mean estimators toward the global minimum for squeezing estimation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational bottleneck in Bayesian QET, proposes a variational framework using polynomial subspaces, provides a geometric interpretation of the solution as an orthogonal projection, derives optimality conditions, and validates the method with single-shot examples of displacement and squeezing estimation.
why_interesting: It provides a practical, analytical tool for optimizing continuous-variable quantum metrology, bridging the gap between complex numerical Bayesian methods and experimentally implementable Gaussian operations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Relative MSL in Eq. (43) as a function of the prior variance σ2 0 for estimating a displacement parameter θ as per Eq. (44). Here, we use a uniform prior with width ∆and variance σ2 0 = ∆2/12, and ρin is a coherent state with α = 0.5(1 + i). The dotted and dashed curves denote the relative MSL of the linear {1, ˆq} and cubic {1, ˆq, ˆq3} subspaces, respectively, representing q-homodyne meas- urements with linear and cubic estimators. The solid curve shows the performance of q-homodyne measurements combined with the PM estimator, which includes contributions from all orders. The grey dot-dashed curve indicates the prior MSL ratio for reference.

Figure 2. Relative MSL in Eq. (43) as a function of the prior variance σ2 0 for a Gaussian prior. Here, the task is estimating the squeezing parameter θ encoded as per Eq. (55), for a vacuum (top) and thermal (bottom) state ρin with ¯n = 0.1. In both plots, the dashed and solid green curves denote the relative MSL for the {1, ˆq2, ˆp2} subspace with the constrained [Eq. (60)] and PM estimator [Eq. (19)] respect- ively.
